course_info.title: Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to VR/AR Hardware and Platforms
learning_objectives: Survey the current landscape of VR and AR hardware
Understand the rendering requirements that distinguish VR from traditional 3D
Compare standalone, tethered, and passthrough VR/AR architectures
readings: Meta Quest Developer Documentation - Getting Started

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual and augmented reality represent a fundamental shift in how humans interact with digital content. Instead of looking at a screen, the user is inside the experience. This changes everything about how we design, render, and think about interactive applications.
key_concepts: VR headset architectures: standalone (Quest 3), tethered (Valve Index), hybrid (PSVR2)
AR device spectrum: phone-based (ARKit/ARCore), headset-based (HoloLens, Vision Pro)
Stereoscopic rendering pipeline: two viewpoints, lens distortion correction
Display technologies: LCD, OLED, micro-OLED, pancake lenses, fresnel lenses
Tracking systems: inside-out (camera-based), outside-in (base stations), hybrid
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Unity XR project targeting Meta Quest, configuring the XR Interaction Toolkit, and deploying a basic scene to a headset with head tracking and passthrough
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2 LTS with XR Plugin Management
Meta Quest 3 or Quest Pro headset in developer mode
Meta XR SDK and XR Interaction Toolkit packages
USB-C cable for deployment and debugging

review.value: Rendering the scene twice from slightly offset eye positions to create depth perception; each eye gets a separate render target that is then corrected for lens distortion before display
Headset cameras observe the environment to determine position and orientation without external sensors; uses visual-inertial odometry combining camera data with IMU readings

class_schedule.topic: Spatial Interaction Design and Hand Tracking
learning_objectives: Design interaction systems for 3D spatial input
Implement hand tracking with gesture recognition
Apply direct and indirect manipulation paradigms in VR
readings: Unity XR Interaction Toolkit - Interactables Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: In VR, the user's hands are the primary interface. Unlike a mouse with 2D coordinates, hands exist in 3D space with full articulation. Designing interactions that feel natural while remaining precise and discoverable is one of the hardest problems in VR development.
key_concepts: Controller-based input: buttons, triggers, thumbsticks, 6DoF tracking
Hand tracking joint data: 26 joints per hand with position and rotation
Gesture recognition: pinch, grab, point, thumbs up, custom gestures
Direct manipulation: grabbing, pushing, rotating objects with virtual hands
Ray-based interaction: far-field selection using pointer rays from hands or controllers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a hand tracking system that supports both pinch-to-grab for nearby objects and ray-based pointing for distant UI panels, with visual feedback showing grab radius and ray intersection
setup_requirements: Meta Quest headset with hand tracking enabled
XR Interaction Toolkit configured for hand tracking
Sample scene with grabbable objects and UI panels
Hand mesh visualization assets

review.value: Bringing the thumb and index finger together; the standard selection gesture in hand tracking because it has clear start/end states, works reliably across hand sizes, and mimics a natural grasping motion
Visual cues that communicate how objects can be manipulated: glow on hover, outline on grab readiness, size pulse on valid targets; critical in VR where there is no cursor to indicate interactability

class_schedule.topic: VR Locomotion Systems and Motion Sickness Mitigation
learning_objectives: Understand the physiological causes of VR-induced motion sickness
Apply evidence-based comfort techniques to reduce nausea
readings: Oculus VR Best Practices Guide - Comfort and Health

lecture_notes.introduction: Moving through virtual space without physically walking creates a sensory conflict between your visual system and vestibular system. This conflict causes motion sickness in many users. Every locomotion choice is a tradeoff between freedom of movement and user comfort.
key_concepts: Teleportation locomotion: arc pointer, blink transition, boundary preview
Continuous locomotion: thumbstick-based, head-directed vs hand-directed
Room-scale movement: guardian boundaries, redirected walking
Comfort techniques: vignetting during movement, snap turning, stable reference frames
Vestibular conflict: why visual motion without physical motion causes nausea
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a locomotion system that offers both teleportation and continuous movement with configurable comfort settings: adjustable vignette intensity, snap turn vs smooth turn toggle, and speed-dependent field-of-view reduction
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with locomotion providers configured
Large virtual environment for testing different locomotion methods
Quest headset with guardian boundary set up
Comfort preference survey form for playtesting

review.value: Dynamically reducing the peripheral field of view during artificial locomotion by fading edges to black; reduces motion sickness because peripheral vision is most sensitive to optic flow that conflicts with vestibular input
Rotating the viewpoint in discrete increments (typically 30-45 degrees) rather than smoothly; eliminates the continuous rotational optic flow that is a primary trigger for VR nausea

class_schedule.topic: 3D User Interfaces in VR and Spatial Audio
learning_objectives: Design and implement user interfaces that exist in 3D virtual space
Apply spatial audio techniques to enhance immersion and provide navigational cues
Integrate HRTF-based audio rendering for accurate sound spatialization
readings: 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice Ch. 7-9

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional 2D UI paradigms break down in VR. Menus cannot hover in screen space because there is no screen. Text must be readable at varying distances. And audio is no longer stereo output - it must exist in 3D space just like visual elements, because your ears are as important as your eyes for spatial presence.
key_concepts: World-space UI panels: curved canvases, follow-head vs fixed-position menus
Text rendering in VR: SDF fonts, minimum angular size for readability
Diegetic UI: interfaces embedded in the game world (wrist watch, clipboard)
HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function): modeling how ears perceive sound direction
Spatial audio techniques: distance attenuation, occlusion, room reverb simulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a diegetic inventory system attached to the player's wrist that opens on forearm inspection, with spatial audio cues that play from item positions in 3D space using Meta Spatial Audio SDK
setup_requirements: Unity project with XR Interaction Toolkit
Meta Spatial Audio SDK installed
TextMeshPro with SDF font assets
Sample audio files for spatialization testing

review.value: A function describing how sound is filtered by the shape of the ears, head, and torso before reaching the eardrums; enables headphone audio to be perceived as coming from specific 3D positions by applying the correct frequency-dependent delays and amplitude differences

User interface elements that exist as objects within the game world rather than as overlaid HUD elements; examples include a wrist-mounted health display or a physical map the character holds; preferred in VR because they maintain immersion and have a natural depth position

class_schedule.topic: AR Marker Tracking and Mixed Reality
learning_objectives: Implement AR marker detection and image tracking
Build mixed reality experiences that blend virtual and physical environments
Use scene understanding APIs to anchor virtual content to real-world surfaces
readings: ARKit Documentation - Image Detection and Tracking
Meta Quest Mixed Reality Developer Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Augmented reality bridges the gap between virtual and physical worlds. Marker tracking gives us precise anchor points to attach virtual content to the real world, while scene understanding lets us place content on surfaces, behind furniture, and interacting with the physical environment.
key_concepts: Image marker detection: reference image databases, tracking quality and distance limits
Plane detection: horizontal and vertical surface recognition and boundary estimation
Scene mesh: real-time 3D reconstruction of the physical environment
Occlusion: virtual objects hidden behind real-world surfaces for realism
Spatial anchors: persistent placement of virtual content that survives across sessions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mixed reality application on Quest 3 that uses passthrough to show the real room, detects a physical poster as an image marker to spawn a 3D character, uses scene mesh for occlusion so the character can hide behind real furniture, and persists the character's position using spatial anchors
setup_requirements: Meta Quest 3 with passthrough enabled
Meta Scene API and Spatial Anchor API configured
Printed image markers for tracking
Room with Scene API room setup completed

review.value: Persistent coordinate references tied to physical locations that survive app restarts and can be shared between devices; the anchor stores the spatial relationship to tracked environmental features so virtual content stays in place even after the device is turned off

Using the real-time 3D mesh of the physical environment as an invisible depth layer so virtual objects are correctly hidden when behind real furniture or walls; requires rendering the scene mesh to the depth buffer without writing to the color buffer

class_schedule.topic: VR/AR Performance Optimization and Platform Deployment
learning_objectives: Optimize VR/AR applications to meet the strict performance requirements of immersive rendering
Apply foveated rendering and other VR-specific optimization techniques
Prepare and submit applications to VR/AR distribution platforms

lecture_notes.introduction: VR has the strictest performance requirements of any real-time application. You must render the scene twice at 72-120Hz with under 20ms total frame time, or the user gets sick. There is no acceptable frame drop in VR - every missed frame is a comfort violation.
key_concepts: VR frame budgets: 13.9ms at 72Hz, 11.1ms at 90Hz, 8.3ms at 120Hz per eye
Fixed foveated rendering: reducing resolution in the peripheral vision of each eye
Application SpaceWarp: generating intermediate frames using motion vectors
Single-pass stereo rendering: rendering both eyes in one draw call
VR-specific profiling: OVR Metrics Tool, GPU-bound vs CPU-bound diagnosis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a VR scene from 45fps to a stable 90fps by enabling single-pass stereo rendering, configuring fixed foveated rendering levels, optimizing draw calls, and using Application SpaceWarp as a safety net for occasional frame drops
setup_requirements: Quest headset with OVR Metrics Tool overlay enabled
Unity project with performance-heavy VR scene
Unity Profiler configured for XR remote profiling
Oculus Developer Hub for sideloading and monitoring

review.value: Rendering the center of each eye at full resolution while reducing peripheral resolution in 2-3 concentric zones; exploits the fact that human vision has much lower acuity in the periphery; can save 20-40% of GPU fragment shading cost with minimal perceived quality loss

A technique where the application renders at half the display refresh rate and the runtime generates intermediate frames by reprojecting using color and depth data with motion vectors; reduces rendering cost by 50% but introduces artifacts on fast-moving objects and disocclusion edges

quizzes.topic: VR Hardware, Interaction Design, and Locomotion

questions.question: A VR developer is building a game that requires the player to explore large outdoor environments. They choose continuous thumbstick locomotion for movement. During playtesting, 60% of testers report moderate nausea within 15 minutes. Describe three specific comfort techniques the developer should implement, explain the physiological reason each one reduces nausea, and discuss any gameplay tradeoffs each introduces.
questions.answer: Three comfort techniques: (1) Dynamic vignetting - darken the peripheral field of view during movement. This works because peripheral vision is most sensitive to optic flow mismatch with the vestibular system. Tradeoff: reduces visual immersion and sense of speed. (2) Snap turning - replace smooth rotation with discrete angular jumps of 30-45 degrees. This eliminates continuous rotational optic flow, which is one of the strongest nausea triggers because the semicircular canals detect no corresponding rotation. Tradeoff: feels less natural and can be disorienting in fast-paced gameplay. (3) Add a stable visual reference frame such as a visible cockpit, nose bridge, or fixed horizon line. This provides the visual system with a stationary reference that matches the vestibular system's report of no movement. Tradeoff: restricts art direction and may not fit all game settings.

questions.question: Explain the difference between direct manipulation and ray-based interaction in VR hand tracking, and describe a specific scenario where each is the better choice.
questions.answer: Direct manipulation means the user physically reaches out and touches or grabs virtual objects with their tracked hands, like picking up a cup. Ray-based interaction projects an invisible or visible ray from the hand to interact with distant objects, like pointing a laser pointer. Direct manipulation is better for near-field tasks like arranging items on a virtual table, assembling parts in a training simulation, or playing a virtual instrument, because it leverages proprioception and feels natural for objects within arm's reach. Ray-based interaction is better for far-field tasks like selecting items on a distant UI menu, pointing at a location on a virtual map across the room, or choosing an object in a large warehouse scene, because the user cannot physically reach distant objects and walking closer breaks workflow.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between the two interaction paradigms
Appropriate scenario for direct manipulation with reasoning
Appropriate scenario for ray-based interaction with reasoning

quizzes.topic: AR Tracking, Mixed Reality, and VR Performance

questions.question: A mixed reality application places a virtual pet on the user's real-world coffee table. When the user walks around the table, the pet sometimes appears to slide on the surface or float above it. Identify two distinct technical causes for this behavior and describe how to fix each one.
questions.answer: Cause 1: Inaccurate plane detection boundary. The detected horizontal plane may not precisely match the table surface, causing a height offset. Fix: Use spatial anchors instead of relying solely on the plane's reported position. Place a spatial anchor at the exact point where the user confirms the pet's position, and update the anchor using scene mesh data for higher geometric accuracy. Cause 2: Tracking drift in the headset's SLAM system. As the user moves, accumulated error in visual-inertial odometry shifts the world coordinate frame. Fix: Enable relocalization by ensuring the environment has sufficient visual features (textured surfaces, varied lighting), and implement anchor persistence so the runtime can correct drift when it re-observes previously seen features. Additionally, snapping the pet's Y position to the scene mesh every frame compensates for small drift amounts.

rubric.key: Identifies plane detection inaccuracy as a cause with valid fix
Identifies tracking drift as a cause with valid fix
Technical depth and specificity of solutions

questions.question: A VR game targeting Quest 3 at 90Hz must complete all rendering in 11.1ms per frame. The developer's scene currently takes 16ms. It is GPU-bound with 350 draw calls and no foveated rendering enabled. Describe a prioritized optimization plan, estimating the millisecond savings from each step.
questions.answer: Prioritized plan: (1) Enable single-pass stereo rendering. This renders both eyes in one pass instead of two, saving nearly all the CPU overhead of duplicated draw calls and reducing GPU vertex processing by up to 30%. Estimated savings: 3-4ms. (2) Enable fixed foveated rendering at medium level. Reduces fragment shading in peripheral zones by approximately 25-30%. Estimated savings: 2-3ms. (3) Reduce draw calls from 350 to under 150 using static batching for non-moving objects and GPU instancing for repeated meshes. This reduces CPU-side draw call overhead and improves GPU state-change efficiency. Estimated savings: 1-2ms. Combined estimated savings: 6-9ms, bringing the frame time from 16ms to approximately 7-10ms, within the 11.1ms budget with headroom for gameplay logic. If still over budget, enable Application SpaceWarp to halve the rendering rate to 45fps with ASW-generated intermediate frames.

rubric.key: Prioritizes single-pass stereo as highest-impact change
Includes foveated rendering with reasonable savings estimate
Addresses draw call reduction with specific techniques
Overall plan is realistic and totals to budget compliance

major_assignments.title: Immersive VR Interaction Experience
major_assignments.description: Design and build a VR experience that demonstrates mastery of spatial interaction design, hand tracking, locomotion, and VR UI. The experience must include at least three distinct interaction mechanics (e.g., grab, point, gesture-triggered action), implement two locomotion options with comfort settings, and feature a diegetic UI element. Must run at 72Hz minimum on Quest hardware.
objectives: Implement hand tracking with reliable gesture recognition for at least two custom gestures
Build a locomotion system with both teleport and continuous options and configurable comfort settings
Design a diegetic UI that conveys information without breaking immersion
Integrate spatial audio for at least three in-world sound sources
deliverables: Unity project deployable to Meta Quest via sideload
APK file tested on physical Quest hardware
Video walkthrough demonstrating all interaction mechanics (3-5 minutes)
Design document explaining interaction design decisions and comfort rationale (3-4 pages)

criteria: Hand tracking gestures are recognized reliably
Grab and release mechanics feel natural
Ray-based interaction provides clear visual feedback
Interaction affordances clearly communicate object interactability
Teleportation works with arc visualization and valid-area feedback
Continuous movement includes configurable vignette and speed options
Snap and smooth turn options are both available
Comfort settings are accessible from an in-VR menu
Audio sources are correctly spatialized in 3D
Diegetic UI is readable and functional
UI interaction uses appropriate input method (hand or ray)
Sound occlusion works when sources are behind objects
Maintains 72Hz on Quest hardware
No visible tracking glitches or hand jitter
Frame time profiling data provided
No loading hitches during gameplay
Polished hand tracking interactions that feel natural and reliable
Thoughtful locomotion with real comfort options
Immersive spatial audio and diegetic UI design
Stable performance on standalone VR hardware

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of VR/AR theory, hardware knowledge, and interaction design principles

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in hand tracking, locomotion, spatial audio, and AR tracking

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive VR interaction experience demonstrating mastery of core immersive technologies

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment covering VR/AR hardware, interaction design, comfort, and spatial concepts

final_project.description: Capstone mixed reality application integrating VR interaction, AR tracking, and spatial computing

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions involving VR/AR hardware. Headset time is limited and shared; missing a lab session cannot be made up without prior arrangement.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. All VR/AR submissions must include a video walkthrough as the instructor may not have immediate headset access for grading.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. VR headsets may be shared for testing but all code and design work must be original. Peer playtesting is encouraged and should be documented.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original or properly attributed. Use of XR template projects must be disclosed and substantially modified. Submitting unmodified SDK samples as original work will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: The department provides Meta Quest headsets for lab use. Students with personal headsets may use them for at-home development. All submissions must target Meta Quest 3 as the reference platform.
